cheese.notes: Match by texture: fresh/soft (cottage cheese, ricotta, cream cheese), creamy (brie, camembert), semifirm (cheddar, gouda, mozzarella, swiss), hard aged (parmesan, gruyere). Substitute within the same category for best results.

cottage cheese.notes: Fresh unripened — interchangeable for soft, wet texture.

cream cheese.notes: Fresh unripened — use ricotta or mascarpone for spreading; cottage cheese for baking.

ricotta.notes: Fresh unripened — drain cottage cheese for a closer texture.

brie.notes: Soft-ripened — interchangeable for creamy melt and mild flavor.

camembert.notes: Soft-ripened — brie is the closest substitute.

cheddar.notes: Semifirm — all melt well. Colby and Monterey Jack are mildest; Swiss is nuttier.

mozzarella.notes: Semifirm — fontina melts best for pasta or pizza; Monterey Jack is mildest.

gouda.notes: Semifirm — edam and havarti are closest in mild, buttery flavor.

parmesan.notes: Hard aged — pecorino is saltier; asiago is milder. Use same quantity.

gruyere.notes: Hard aged — swiss or emmental are the easiest to find and melt similarly.

swiss.notes: Semifirm — jarlsberg is the closest in flavor and melt.

milk.notes: Use equal amounts. To thicken milk to mimic half-and-half, add 1 tablespoon cornstarch or flour per cup.

half-and-half.notes: Thicken 1 cup milk with 1 tablespoon cornstarch or flour, or thin heavy cream with a splash of water.

heavy cream.notes: For 1 cup: use 3/4 cup milk + 1/4 cup melted butter, or thicken 1 cup milk with 1-2 tablespoons cornstarch. Note: dairy alternatives won't whip into whipped cream.

buttermilk.notes: For 1 cup: add 1 tablespoon lemon juice or white vinegar to a cup, then fill to 1 cup with milk. Or thin yogurt or sour cream with equal parts milk.

butter.notes: For frying/cooking: use olive oil or other neutral oils. For flavor (risotto, polenta): heavy cream or mascarpone work well.

sour cream.notes: Creamy dairy — all are interchangeable for tang and texture.

creme fraiche.notes: Creamy dairy — sour cream is the closest substitute.

mascarpone.notes: Creamy dairy — cream cheese is slightly tangier; whisk with a little cream to soften.

yogurt.notes: Creamy dairy — thin with a little water or milk if needed for liquid recipes.

substitutes: flax egg (1 tbsp ground flax + 3 tbsp water)
egg.notes: For baking only (1 large egg = 50g): fruit purees add moisture and subtle flavor; aquafaba (chickpea liquid) gives springy results; flax egg gives fluffiest results. These don't replace eggs in fried or scrambled preparations.

substitutes: flax egg (1 tbsp ground flax + 3 tbsp water)
eggs.notes: For baking only (1 large egg = 50g): fruit purees add moisture and subtle flavor; aquafaba (chickpea liquid) gives springy results; flax egg gives fluffiest results.

beef.notes: Match cut type: tough cuts (chuck, brisket) for other tough cuts; tender cuts (strip, filet) for other quick-cooking cuts. Lamb has a more assertive flavor.

ground beef.notes: All interchangeable. Lean meats (chicken, turkey) may need extra oil added for fat content.

ground meat.notes: Ground meats are interchangeable. Add oil for leaner substitutes. Sausage can sub in — just remove from casing first.

substitutes: ground meat seasoned with red-pepper flakes, fennel seed, Italian herbs
sausage.notes: Remove sausage from casing and cook as ground meat, or season ground meat with sausage spices.

pork.notes: Bone-in pork chops cook like steaks of similar thickness. Cubed beef stew meat works at similar rate; cubed chicken needs reduced cook time.

chicken.notes: Butterfly or pound chicken breasts to match thigh thickness. Ground turkey works for ground chicken recipes.

shrimp.notes: Shrimp cook very quickly — match with other quick-cooking proteins. Adjust time for larger substitutes.

substitutes: lean: bass, cod, halibut, tilapia, snapper
fish.notes: Substitute lean for lean, fatty for fatty. Most fish fillets cook at similar rates.

salmon.notes: Fatty fish — all are interchangeable in most recipes.

cod.notes: Lean fish — all are interchangeable in most recipes.

olive oil.notes: Medium-high smoke point — avocado oil is closest in flavor. For high-heat cooking use canola or vegetable oil instead.

vegetable oil.notes: Neutral high-smoke-point oils — all interchangeable for frying and baking.

canola oil.notes: Neutral oil — all are interchangeable at equal quantities.

coconut oil.notes: High smoke point neutral oil — use equal amounts. Butter works well for flavor-forward recipes.

lard.notes: Solid fat — all have similar low smoke points. Butter adds flavor; shortening is more neutral.

stock.notes: Stock primarily adds liquid. For small amounts use water. For large amounts, season water with soy sauce, miso, wine, or beer to taste.

chicken stock.notes: Use equal amounts. Season water substitutes to taste — start small.

beef stock.notes: Dark, rich flavor — layer soy sauce and tomato paste in water for depth.

vegetable stock.notes: Mildest stock — water with miso or mushroom liquid adds umami depth.

spinach.notes: Mild tender green — all interchangeable. Add later in cooking as they wilt quickly.

kale.notes: Bitter firm green — add earlier in cooking. Collard greens are closest in texture.

arugula.notes: Bitter tender green — watercress is peppery and closest; all work raw or briefly wilted.

cabbage.notes: Mild firm green — all are interchangeable for cooked dishes.

broccoli.notes: Quick-cooking dense vegetable — all cook at similar rates.

cauliflower.notes: Quick-cooking — all interchangeable. Cauliflower is milder and firmer than broccoli.

zucchini.notes: Quick-cooking light vegetable — all have similar cook times and mild flavor.

mushrooms.notes: Quick-cooking umami ingredient — eggplant is closest in meatiness.

potato.notes: Slow-cooking dense vegetable — all need similar roasting/boiling times.

sweet potato.notes: Dense sweet vegetable — butternut squash is closest in sweetness and texture.

carrot.notes: Slow-cooking root vegetable — parsnip is closest in flavor and texture.

onion.notes: All alliums are largely interchangeable. Dried onion powder is more potent — use sparingly.

garlic.notes: Garlic's pronounced flavor makes exact substitution hard. Garlic powder (1/4 tsp = 1 clove) is most direct; shallots add mild sweetness.

shallot.notes: Milder and sweeter than onion — use slightly less onion as a substitute.

leek.notes: Mild allium — onion works well; use less as it's more pungent.

basil.notes: Tender bright herb — swap 1:1 with other tender herbs.

bay leaves.notes: Woody herb — use any combination of woody herbs as a substitute.

cilantro.notes: Tender bright herb — parsley is the most neutral substitute for those who dislike cilantro.

parsley.notes: Tender bright herb — most neutral of the group; any tender herb works.

rosemary.notes: Woody savory herb — thyme is closest. Use 1 tsp dried for 1 tbsp fresh.

thyme.notes: Woody savory herb — oregano is closest. Use 1 tsp dried for 1 tbsp fresh.

oregano.notes: Woody herb — marjoram is milder and closest. Use 1 tsp dried for 1 tbsp fresh.

sage.notes: Woody herb with earthy flavor — rosemary and thyme are closest.

mint.notes: Tender bright herb — basil is closest in freshness. Use sparingly.

dill.notes: Tender bright herb — chervil is closest; parsley is most widely available.

chives.notes: Tender herb with mild onion flavor — scallion greens are closest.

tarragon.notes: Tender anise-flavored herb — chervil is closest; use parsley for neutral flavor.

cinnamon.notes: Warm spice — allspice gives the closest flavor; use a pinch of nutmeg + cloves as a blend.

nutmeg.notes: Warm spice — allspice or a mix of cinnamon and cloves work well.

cumin.notes: Earthy warm spice — coriander is closest; chili powder adds heat. Use slightly less as substitutes can be more pungent.

paprika.notes: Use smoked paprika substitute for smoky dishes. Cayenne is much hotter — use 1/4 the amount.

cayenne.notes: Peppery spice — red-pepper flakes are most direct; hot sauce adds liquid so adjust recipe accordingly.

turmeric.notes: Earthy spice — curry powder is closest and usually contains turmeric. Mainly adds color; saffron also adds color but is much more expensive.

ginger.notes: Peppery warm spice — ground ginger is most direct (use 1/4 the amount of fresh). Allspice and cinnamon give warmth without the bite.

allspice.notes: Warm spice — combine equal pinches of cinnamon, cloves and nutmeg to replicate allspice.

cardamom.notes: Floral spice — coriander and ginger together approximate cardamom's complexity.

cloves.notes: Warm intense spice — use sparingly; allspice is closest.

coriander.notes: Floral earthy spice — cumin is most widely available substitute.

substitutes: paprika + cumin + garlic powder + oregano + cayenne
chili powder.notes: Blend spice — make your own by combining paprika, cumin, garlic powder, oregano and a pinch of cayenne.

white wine.notes: Light alcohol — stock is most neutral; apple juice adds sweetness; diluted white wine vinegar (1 tsp per 1/4 cup liquid) adds acidity.

red wine.notes: Dark alcohol — pomegranate or cranberry juice mimics the depth and color. Dilute vinegar (1 tsp per 1/4 cup) for acidity.

beer.notes: Light beer: use stock or ginger ale. Dark beer: use beef stock or mushroom stock for depth.

substitutes: rice wine vinegar diluted with water
sake.notes: Light alcohol — dry white wine is closest; dilute rice wine vinegar 3:1 with water if needed.

vanilla extract.notes: Use equal amount of vanilla bean paste. Almond extract is more intense — use half. Maple syrup or honey add sweetness.

flour.notes: Wheat flour is the most direct substitute. All-purpose flour is the most versatile. Bread flour has more protein; cake flour is lighter. Almond or oat flour for gluten-free.

all-purpose flour.notes: Wheat flour is the most direct substitute — use equal amounts. Bread flour has more protein; cake flour is lighter.

sugar.notes: Brown sugar adds molasses flavor. For liquid sweeteners (honey, maple syrup) reduce other liquids in recipe slightly.

substitutes: white sugar + molasses (1 tbsp per cup)
brown sugar.notes: Make your own: mix 1 cup white sugar with 1 tablespoon molasses.

substitutes: 1/4 tsp baking soda + 1/2 tsp cream of tartar (per 1 tsp baking powder)
baking powder.notes: For 1 tsp baking powder: use 1/4 tsp baking soda + 1/2 tsp cream of tartar.

baking soda.notes: For 1 tsp baking soda use 3 tsp baking powder — but this adds extra sodium and may affect flavor.

lemon juice.notes: Lime juice is the most direct substitute. Vinegar is more acidic — use half the amount.

soy sauce.notes: All add umami and saltiness. Coconut aminos is less salty; fish sauce is more intense — use half.

tomato paste.notes: Tomato paste is concentrated — simmer tomato sauce down or use 3 tablespoons sauce per 1 tablespoon paste.

breadcrumbs.notes: All provide crunchy coating or binding. Panko gives the crispiest results.
